section.title: Suffering for Righteousness
section.sectionRationale: Peter gathers the household teaching into a call for the whole church to unity and blessing, then addresses suffering for righteousness directly: be ready to give a reason for your hope, and remember that Christ suffered for sins once for all. The passage rises to the letter's most debated verses on Christ preaching to the spirits in prison and the baptism that now saves.
section.thesis: Because Christ suffered once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God, and now reigns victorious over every power, believers can bless those who wrong them, suffer for righteousness without fear, and always be ready to give a gentle, reverent reason for the hope within them.
section.frameNote: Three movements: unity and blessing instead of retaliation, verses 8 to 12; suffering for righteousness and giving a defense, verses 13 to 17; and Christ's victorious suffering, the spirits in prison, and baptism, verses 18 to 22.

verses.text: Finally, all of you, be {{homophrones|like-minded}} and sympathetic, love as brothers, be tenderhearted and humble.
Do not repay evil with evil or insult with insult, but with {{eulogia|blessing}}, because to this you were called so that you may inherit a blessing.
For, “Whoever would love life and see good days must keep his tongue from evil and his lips from deceitful speech.
He must turn from evil and do good; he must seek peace and pursue it.
For the eyes of the Lord are on the righteous, and His ears are inclined to their prayer. But the face of the Lord is against those who do evil.”

units.label: Suffering for righteousness, ready to answer
units.note: give a defense for your hope

verses.text: Who can harm you if you are zealous for what is good?
But even if you should suffer for what is right, you are blessed. “Do not fear what they fear; do not be shaken.”
But in your hearts sanctify Christ as Lord. Always be prepared to give a {{apologia|defense}} to everyone who asks you the reason for the hope that is in you. But respond with gentleness and respect,
keeping a clear {{syneidesis|conscience}}, so that those who slander you may be put to shame by your good behavior in Christ.
For it is better, if it is God’s will, to suffer for doing good than for doing evil.
For Christ also suffered for sins once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring you to God. He was put to death in the body but {{zoopoieo|made alive}} in the Spirit,
in whom He also went and preached to the {{phylake|spirits in prison}}
who disobeyed long ago when God waited patiently in the days of Noah while the ark was being built. In the ark a few people, only eight souls, were saved through water.

And this water symbolizes the {{baptisma|baptism}} that now saves you also, not the removal of dirt from the body, but the pledge of a clear conscience toward God, through the resurrection of Jesus Christ,
who has gone into heaven and is at the right hand of God, with angels, authorities, and powers subject to Him.

homophrones.gloss: Of one mind, like-minded, sharing a common outlook. Peter heads his list of church virtues with it: believers are to think and feel together in harmony. It does not mean uniformity of opinion but a shared mind shaped by the gospel, the ground of sympathy, brotherly love, tenderheartedness, and humility.

eulogia.gloss: Blessing, the speaking of good. Instead of repaying insult with insult, believers are to bless, and in doing so they inherit a blessing themselves. The word turns the natural cycle of retaliation on its head: those who have received God's blessing pass blessing on, even to those who wrong them.

apologia.gloss: A defense, a reasoned answer, as one might give in court. Believers are to be always ready to give an apologia for their hope. It is not aggressive argument but a thoughtful, gentle account of why they trust Christ, offered with respect to anyone who asks. From this word comes apologetics.

syneidesis.gloss: Conscience, the inner sense of right and wrong before God. Peter twice ties suffering and baptism to a clear or good conscience. A clean conscience, kept by godly conduct, both silences slander and marks the reality of salvation, the heart at peace with God through Christ's resurrection.

zoopoieo.gloss: To make alive, to give life. Christ was put to death in the body but made alive in the Spirit. The word points to the resurrection life and power of the Spirit by which the crucified Christ was raised and vindicated, the same power in which He proclaimed His triumph and now reigns.

phylake.gloss: Spirits in prison, those held in confinement. This much-debated phrase refers either to the disobedient people of Noah's day, now dead, or to imprisoned fallen spirits from that era. In whatever sense, Christ went and proclaimed His victory to them, showing His triumph reaches even to the powers of the disobedient past.

baptisma.gloss: Baptism, the washing that marks entrance into Christ. Peter says it saves not by removing bodily dirt but as the pledge of a clear conscience toward God, and only through the resurrection of Jesus Christ. Baptism saves as it unites the believer to the risen Lord, its power resting in Christ, not the water itself.

basic.context: Peter calls the whole church to live in harmony: be united, sympathetic, loving, tenderhearted, humble. Do not hit back when insulted; bless instead, and you will inherit blessing. Even if you suffer for doing right, you are blessed, so do not be afraid. Instead, honor Christ as Lord in your heart, and always be ready to explain your hope, gently and respectfully, with a clear conscience. Then Peter grounds it all in Christ, who suffered once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God. He died, was made alive, proclaimed His victory even to the spirits in prison, and now reigns in heaven with all powers under Him. Baptism, like Noah's rescue through water, saves us through His resurrection.
basic.hermeneutics: Most of this passage is plain and practical, but verses 18 to 22 contain some of the hardest lines in the New Testament, especially Christ preaching to the spirits in prison. Faithful Christians have read these differently, and we should hold our view humbly while keeping the clear main point: Christ's suffering was victorious and complete, and He now reigns over every power. Likewise, when Peter says baptism now saves you, he immediately guards against superstition, it is not the water but the resurrection of Christ and the appeal of a clean conscience that saves.
basic.originalLanguages: Believers are homophrones, like-minded, and return eulogia, blessing, for insult. They give an apologia, a reasoned defense, of their hope with a clear syneidesis, conscience. Christ was put to death but zoopoieo, made alive, in the Spirit, and proclaimed His triumph to the pneumata en phylake, the spirits in prison. Baptisma, baptism, saves through the resurrection, as the pledge of a good conscience.
basic.typology: Noah's flood is the great type here: as eight souls were saved through water in the ark, so baptism saves through the waters of judgment by union with Christ. The flood pictures judgment passed through into new life. And Christ's descent and proclamation, and His exaltation over all powers, fulfil the hope that God's righteous one triumphs over every hostile force.
basic.christ: Christ is the heart of the passage: He suffered for sins once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God, the clearest statement of substitution in the letter. He was put to death and made alive, proclaimed His victory to the imprisoned spirits, rose, ascended, and now sits at God's right hand with angels, authorities, and powers subject to Him. He is the suffering Savior and the reigning Lord.
basic.god: God is patient, as He was in the days of Noah, waiting while the ark was built. He is the God whose eyes are on the righteous and whose ears hear their prayer, but whose face is against evildoers. He is the God to whom Christ brings us, and who raised and exalted Christ over every power. He is just, patient, attentive, and sovereign over all.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 34 is quoted on loving life and seeking peace, and God's care for the righteous. Isaiah 8 lies behind sanctify Christ as Lord and do not fear. Genesis 6 to 8 gives Noah and the flood. And Romans 8 declares that no power can separate us from Christ, who reigns over all.
basic.oneStory: From Noah saved through the flood to Christ raised and enthroned over every power, God's story moves through judgment to rescue and triumph. Christ's once-for-all suffering brings sinners to God, and His resurrection is the power in which baptism now saves. The suffering church, blessing its enemies and ready to testify, lives under a Lord who has already conquered and reigns over all.

context.literary: Verse 8 draws the household section to a close with finally, all of you, listing communal virtues, then supports the call with a long quotation from Psalm 34. Verses 13 to 17 apply the theme to suffering for righteousness. Verses 18 to 22 ground everything in a compressed, hymn-like passage on Christ's death, vindication, descent or proclamation, resurrection, and exaltation, ending on His universal reign.
context.historical: The readers faced slander and the threat of suffering simply for being Christians. The call to be ready with a gentle defense fits a setting of informal accusation and questioning rather than only formal trials. The flood account and the fate of the disobedient spirits were part of Jewish tradition, including texts like 1 Enoch, which color the imagery Peter uses in verses 19 to 20.
context.canonical: The passage contains one of the New Testament's key atonement statements, the righteous for the unrighteous, and its most disputed verses on Christ and the spirits in prison. It has shaped the creedal line He descended, and it is a central text on baptism. It also joins the Bible's theme of Christ's exaltation over all rule and authority.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call the church to unity, blessing, and fearless, gentle witness under suffering, and to anchor that call in the once-for-all suffering, vindication, and universal reign of Christ, so that believers who suffer for righteousness know they follow a Savior who has already triumphed over every power.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 8 to 17 are largely prescriptive: be united, bless, do not fear, be ready to answer, keep a clear conscience. Verses 18 to 22 turn to description, the saving work and exaltation of Christ, which grounds the commands. The atonement and reign of Christ are to be trusted; the resulting conduct is to be lived.

debates.question: What does Christ preaching to the spirits in prison mean, verses 18 to 20?

views.label: Christ proclaiming victory to imprisoned fallen spirits
views.case: Many take the spirits as the rebellious angelic beings associated with Noah's generation, now imprisoned. After His death and resurrection Christ announced His triumph over them, so the passage declares His victory over hostile powers, fitting the exaltation over angels and authorities in verse 22.

views.label: Christ preaching through Noah to that generation
views.case: Following Augustine, others hold that the pre-incarnate Christ, by His Spirit, preached through Noah to the disobedient people of that day, who are now spirits in prison. The point is God's long patience and the warning of judgment for the unbelieving.

views.label: Christ proclaiming to the dead of Noah's day
views.case: A third orthodox reading sees Christ, between death and resurrection, proclaiming to the departed spirits of Noah's contemporaries, whether as announcement of triumph or of their condemnation, without teaching a second chance for salvation.

debates.bearing: The views differ on the who and when, but all orthodox readings agree on the main point: Christ's death was victorious, not a defeat, and His triumph reaches even to the disobedient past. None teaches a second chance after death. Certainty on the details is less important than the clear message of Christ's victory.

debates.question: What does Peter mean that baptism now saves you, verse 21?

views.label: Baptism saves as it unites us to the risen Christ
views.case: Peter at once explains: not the removal of dirt from the body, but the pledge of a clear conscience toward God, through the resurrection of Jesus Christ. Baptism saves instrumentally, as the God-given sign and seal of union with the risen Lord, its power resting entirely in Christ.

views.label: Baptism as the outward figure of an inward reality
views.case: Others stress that the water symbolizes; the saving reality is the appeal to God from a good conscience and the resurrection of Christ. Baptism saves in a figurative or representative sense, standing for the salvation it signifies, never by the water itself.

debates.bearing: Both readings reject any notion that the water automatically saves. Peter guards against superstition in the same breath: it is Christ's resurrection and a conscience made clean before God, received by faith, that save, with baptism as their appointed sign.

study.originalLanguages: Peter calls the church to be homophrones, like-minded, tenderhearted, and humble, returning eulogia, blessing, for insult, and so inheriting blessing. Under suffering they sanctify Christ as Lord and stand ready to give an apologia, a reasoned defense, of their hope, with gentleness, respect, and a clear syneidesis, conscience. The ground is Christ, who suffered for sins once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, put to death in the body but zoopoieo, made alive, in the Spirit. In that power He went and proclaimed to the pneumata en phylake, the spirits in prison, from Noah's day, and now reigns exalted over every power. Baptisma, baptism, saves not by washing the body but as the pledge of a good conscience toward God, through the resurrection of Jesus Christ.

typology.type: Noah and the eight saved through water
typology.fulfillment: Baptism, which saves through the waters of judgment by union with the risen Christ, as the ark carried Noah's family through the flood into new life.

typology.type: Christ's descent and proclamation to imprisoned spirits
typology.fulfillment: The victory of God's righteous one over every hostile power, announced even to the disobedient of the ancient past.

typology.type: The patient waiting of God in Noah's day
typology.fulfillment: God's continued patience in the gospel age, holding open the door of salvation before the final judgment.

study.christ: Christ is both the suffering Savior and the reigning Lord in this passage. His death is the clearest atonement statement in the letter: He suffered for sins once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God. He was put to death yet made alive, and in that risen power He proclaimed His triumph even to the spirits in prison. He rose, ascended, and now sits at God's right hand with angels, authorities, and powers subject to Him. The Christ whom suffering believers follow has already conquered every power that could threaten them.
study.god: God is revealed as patient, just, attentive, and sovereign. He waited patiently in the days of Noah, and His patience still holds open the day of salvation. His eyes are on the righteous and His ears open to their prayer, while His face is set against evildoers. He is the God to whom Christ brings us through His atoning death, and the God who raised and exalted Christ above every power. He hears, He judges, and He reigns over all through His Son.

crossReferences.note: Quoted in verses 10 to 12 on loving life, seeking peace, and God's care for the righteous over against evildoers.
Do not fear what they fear, but sanctify the LORD, the source of Peter's call not to be shaken but to sanctify Christ as Lord.
Noah, the ark, and the flood, the account behind the spirits in prison and the water that prefigures baptism.
Christ raised and interceding at God's right hand, from whom no power can separate us, matching Christ's exaltation over all authorities.

study.oneStory: God's story runs through judgment to rescue and triumph. In Noah's day He saved a few through the flood; in Christ He saves through the once-for-all suffering of the righteous for the unrighteous. Christ died, was made alive, proclaimed His victory even to the imprisoned spirits of the ancient past, and now reigns over every power. Baptism marks His people's passage through judgment into new life by union with the risen Lord. The suffering church blesses its enemies and testifies to its hope because its Savior has already conquered and sits enthroned over all.

quiz.q: How are believers to respond when insulted or wronged?

options.text: With blessing rather than retaliation, since they were called to inherit a blessing.
options.note: Verse 9: repay evil and insult with blessing.

options.text: By repaying insult with insult to defend their honor.
options.note: Peter forbids repaying evil with evil or insult with insult.

options.text: By quietly withdrawing from all who oppose them.
options.note: The call is active blessing, not mere withdrawal.

options.text: By returning good for evil as those who have been blessed.
options.note: True to the sense, though the answer should name blessing and the inheritance of blessing.

quiz.q: What does Peter say believers should always be ready to do?

options.text: Give a gentle, respectful defense of the reason for their hope.
options.note: Verse 15: be prepared to answer, with gentleness and respect.

options.text: Win every argument against those who oppose them.
options.note: The answer is gentle and respectful, not combative victory.

options.text: Stay silent so as not to provoke opposition.
options.note: Peter calls for a ready, gentle answer, not silence.

options.text: Explain their faith when questioned, honoring Christ as Lord.
options.note: True and close, though the answer should include gentleness and respect.

quiz.q: How does Peter describe the meaning of Christ's suffering in verse 18?

options.text: He suffered for sins once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God.
options.note: Verse 18: the letter's clearest statement of substitutionary atonement.

options.text: He suffered merely as an example of endurance.
options.note: He is our example elsewhere, but here the stress is substitution to bring us to God.

options.text: He suffered as a tragic accident of history.
options.note: His suffering was purposeful, once for all, to reconcile us to God.

options.text: He suffered to reconcile sinners to God once for all.
options.note: True to the sense, though the answer should include the righteous for the unrighteous.

quiz.q: What is the main point agreed by orthodox readings of Christ preaching to the spirits in prison?

options.text: That Christ's death was victorious, His triumph reaching even to the disobedient past.
options.note: Verses 18 to 22: however the details are read, Christ's victory and reign are the point.

options.text: That people get a second chance to be saved after death.
options.note: No orthodox reading teaches a second chance after death.

options.text: That Christ was defeated and imprisoned for a time.
options.note: The opposite: He proclaims triumph and is exalted over all powers.

options.text: That Christ now reigns with all powers subject to Him.
options.note: True, verse 22, and part of the point, though the disputed verses stress His victorious proclamation.

quiz.q: In what sense does baptism now save you, according to verse 21?

options.text: Not by washing the body, but as the pledge of a clear conscience, through Christ's resurrection.
options.note: Verse 21 guards against superstition: the power is Christ's resurrection, not the water.

options.text: By the water itself automatically removing sin.
options.note: Peter explicitly denies it is the removal of dirt from the body.

options.text: As the outward sign of the salvation received through faith in the risen Christ.
options.note: True to the figurative reading, though the answer should name the pledge of a clear conscience.

options.text: As a ritual that earns God's favor by being performed.
options.note: Baptism saves through union with the risen Christ, not as a meritorious act.

questions: Peter calls the whole church to be like-minded, sympathetic, and humble. Where is God asking you to pursue unity and tenderness rather than being right?
Instead of repaying insult with insult, we are to bless. Who has wronged you, and what would it look like to speak a genuine blessing over them?
Are you ready to give a gentle, respectful reason for your hope? How would you put into words why you trust Christ if someone asked you today?
Christ suffered, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring you to God. Sit with that exchange for a moment. How does it steady you when you suffer for doing right?
Christ now reigns with every power subject to Him. What fear or hostile force in your life shrinks when you remember who is on the throne?

translationNotes.note: Sanctify Christ as Lord echoes Isaiah 8:13; some manuscripts read sanctify the Lord God. Give a defense translates apologia, a reasoned answer offered with gentleness and respect.
Made alive in the Spirit and in whom He also went can be rendered made alive in the spirit, in which He went; the precise sense shapes the debated reading of the spirits in prison.

The clause not the removal of dirt from the body, but the pledge of a clear conscience toward God is set off by commas here; pledge can also be rendered appeal, an appeal to God from a good conscience.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Peter 3:8-22. Em dashes in verse 21 were rendered as commas per the no-dash rule, preserving wording.
